planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 9–11; Hainan Fan Palm is a small to medium, shade-tolerant, clustering fan palm endemic to the tropical rainforests of Hainan Island, China — producing dense clusters of slender stems topped by circular, deeply divided, bright-green fan fronds, and among the more cold-tolerant of tropical clustering fan palms.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Chuniophoenix hainanensis prefers the moist, shaded conditions of its native forest understory — protect from direct sun, cold below 26°F, and drought; mulch deeply to maintain cool, moist root zone. Chuniophoenix hainanensis (Hainan Fan Palm) is a small, clustering, forest-understory fan palm endemic to the tropical rainforests of Hainan Island, China — a biodiversity hotspot with numerous endemic species. The genus Chuniophoenix contains only two species, both endemic to southern China. The Hainan Fan Palm forms dense clusters of slender, reed-like stems topped by attractively divided circular fan fronds with individual segments hanging gracefully. Unlike most fan palms which prefer full sun, Chuniophoenix hainanensis is genuinely shade-adapted and performs best in partial to deep shade, reflecting its position as a forest understory specialist. The palm has gained attention from palm enthusiasts for its clustering habit, shade tolerance, moderate cold hardiness (surviving to zone 9a with protection), and compact size suitable for smaller garden spaces. Small, round, orange-red fruits appear periodically on female plants. Listed as Vulnerable in the wild due to habitat loss on Hainan; always purchase nursery-grown specimens.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in full sun in 6–18 inches of water, in aquatic pots with heavy clay loam and slow-release aquatic fertilizer tablets in zone 9–12; Blue Lotus (Blue Water Lily of the Nile) is a stunning tropical aquatic from Egypt, the Nile Basin, and East Africa — sacred in ancient Egyptian religion and art — bearing beautiful, sky-blue to pale-lavender, star-shaped, fragrant flowers that open in the morning on floating pads in full sun above still or gently moving water.

directions: Plant tubers in aquatic containers with heavy clay loam (no potting mix — it floats) and aquatic fertilizer tablets, submerged 6–18 inches deep in full sun; Nymphaea caerulea requires warm water (above 65°F) and full sun (minimum 6 hours) to bloom — overwinter tubers indoors in cold climates or treat as an annual. Nymphaea caerulea (Blue Lotus, Blue Water Lily, or Egyptian Blue Lotus) is a tropical, day-blooming aquatic water lily native to the Nile River and associated waterways of Egypt, Sudan, and East Africa, one of the most historically and culturally significant flowers in human civilization. The Blue Lotus was the sacred flower of ancient Egypt — appearing in thousands of tomb paintings, bas reliefs, hieroglyphics, and temple carvings across 3,000+ years of Egyptian religious art, where it symbolized the sun, rebirth, and creation. The flower is botanically distinctive: sky-blue to lavender-blue, star-shaped, with a yellow center and a delicate, sweet, tea-like fragrance. It blooms in the morning sun and closes in the afternoon. Nymphaea caerulea was used in ancient Egyptian ritual and festival use — the flowers were floated in water, worn as garlands, and appear to have been used in traditional pharmacological preparations for their mildly psychoactive aporphine alkaloid content. In modern warm-climate pond gardening (zone 9–12), the Blue Lotus is one of the most beautiful and historical of all aquatic ornamentals, growing vigorously in full-sun ponds and water features. Store tubers above 55°F indoors over winter in colder climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Tropical Water Lilies
Encyclopedia of Ancient Egyptian Plants — Germer (1985)

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil or shallow water (2–12 inches deep) in zone 9–12; Papyrus is a large, dramatic, aquatic sedge native to the Nile Basin and sub-Saharan Africa — the plant from which the ancient Egyptians produced the world's first writing surface (papyrus paper) — producing tall, triangular stems topped by spectacular, spherical, radiating umbels of fine, thread-like bracts resembling giant green fireworks.

directions: Plant in full sun in consistently wet, fertile soil or shallow water (2–12 inches deep) in pots set in ponds, bog gardens, or rain gardens; Cyperus papyrus is a water-loving plant that must never dry out — grow at the edge of ponds, in aquatic containers in water features, or in persistently moist bog areas with at least 6 hours of full sun. Cyperus papyrus (Papyrus or Egyptian Papyrus) is a tall, dramatic, rhizomatous, aquatic sedge native to the margins of the Nile River, Lake Chad, and the shallow freshwater marshes, lakeshores, and riverbanks of tropical Africa from Egypt south through East Africa to Madagascar. It is one of the most historically significant plant species in human civilization: the stems of Cyperus papyrus provided the material from which ancient Egyptians manufactured papyrus paper — the world's first practical writing and documentation surface, produced from thin, crisscrossed strips of the pith — enabling the record-keeping and administrative systems that underlie ancient Egyptian, Greek, and early Roman civilization. The triangular, 3-sided stems (typical of the sedge family, hence the field-guide maxim 'sedges have edges') are hollow, spongy, and grow to 5–12 feet tall, topped by dramatic spherical umbels of 100+ fine, radiating, thread-like stems with tiny brown flower clusters — the visual effect is of enormous green fireworks or celestial spheres. Ornamentally outstanding as a bold water-garden or pond accent in zone 9–12; can become invasive in year-round warm climates where it naturalized beyond the garden.
sources: The British Museum — Papyrus from Plant to Paper

planting.note: Divide rhizomes and plant in fall or spring in full sun to partial shade in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 6–10; Dixie Iris is the largest-flowered native iris of the Gulf Coast states — Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Florida Panhandle, and South Carolina — producing spectacular, large, blue-violet to red-purple flowers in spring at pond edges, drainage ditches, bayous, and moist meadows where it is tolerant of prolonged flooding and alkaline as well as acidic soils.

directions: Plant rhizomes in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade — Iris hexagona is adapted to water-saturated soils and tolerates several weeks of complete flooding; excellent for rain gardens, pond margins, wet ditches, and boggy areas. Iris hexagona (Dixie Iris or Zigzag Iris) is a large-flowered, robust, native beardless iris indigenous to the coastal wetlands, bayous, river margins, brackish marshes, and wet prairie soils of the Gulf Coast states — Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Arkansas, Florida, Georgia, and South Carolina — and one of the most spectacular native wetland wildflowers of the Deep South. The large, showy flowers (3–4 inches across) are produced in spring in shades of deep blue-violet to rich red-purple, sometimes with yellow signals, held on sturdy, zigzag-branched stalks (hence one common name 'Zigzag Iris') above glossy, strap-like foliage reaching 3–4 feet. Iris hexagona is the dominant native parent species in the development of Louisiana Irises (Iris × louisiana hybrids) — the largest-flowered, most diverse, and most adaptable of all American native iris groups, created by hybridization with I. fulva, I. brevicaulis, and I. nelsonii. Unlike most garden irises, Dixie Iris thrives in wet, boggy, poorly drained, even flooded soils and tolerates wide pH variation. Excellent for Gulf Coast rain gardens, pond edges, and natural wet-area planting.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Iris hexagona

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring (necks at soil level) in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 7–11; Florida Swamp Lily is a spectacular, large-flowered, native Crinum bulb indigenous to the wetlands, swamp margins, bayous, and river edges of the Gulf Coast from Texas to Florida — producing clusters of large, fragrant, white, spider-lily-like flowers all summer from enormous bulbs adapted to standing water and periodic flooding.

directions: Plant large bulbs with necks at or just below soil surface in consistently moist to wet, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — Florida Swamp Lily is adapted to saturated, flooded soils and should be placed at pond edges, in rain gardens, or in any consistently moist to wet position; never allow to dry out in summer. Crinum americanum (Florida Swamp Lily or American Swamp Lily) is a large, robustly growing, bulbous perennial native to the freshwater and slightly brackish marshes, swamp margins, bayou edges, and river shores of the Gulf Coastal Plain from Texas east through Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, and Florida — one of the most dramatic and handsome native wetland flowers of the southeastern United States. The large, clustered, white, fragrant, spider-lily-like flowers — each bloom with 6 long, narrow, straplike petals and long, projecting stamens — are produced in umbels of 3–9 on long, stout, hollow scapes reaching 2–4 feet, blooming repeatedly through summer and fall. The enormous, onion-like bulbs adapt remarkably well to standing water conditions — the plants naturally grow with their lower bulb sections submerged — and tolerate both freshwater and slight salinity. Crinum americanum is closely related to the widely planted C. asiaticum (Giant Crinum) but is native to the Americas and naturally cold-hardy through zone 7. An outstanding, floriferous, low-maintenance native plant for Gulf Coast wet gardens, pond margins, and rain gardens. Do not confuse with the invasive Crinum bulbispermum.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Crinum americanum
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Crinum americanum

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with necks at soil surface in moist to well-drained, sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 8–11; Coastal Spider Lily is a robust, salt-tolerant native bulb of the coastal scrubs, shell mounds, and beach margins of Florida and the Caribbean — producing large, spectacular, pure-white, intensely fragrant spider lily flowers on tall stems throughout summer — among the most salt-tolerant and coastal-adapted of all native Florida bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs with necks at or just above soil surface in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Hymenocallis latifolia is tolerant of coastal conditions — salt spray, limestone soils, and periodic drought once established — an excellent native bulb for coastal gardens and sandy, alkaline-soil landscapes in zone 8–11. Hymenocallis latifolia (Coastal Spider Lily or Perfumed Spider Lily) is a large, robust, bulbous perennial native to the coastal scrubs, hammocks, rocky pinelands, shell mounds, and beach margin habitats of southern Florida, the Florida Keys, and the Caribbean islands. The large, pure-white, intensely fragrant flowers — the characteristic Hymenocallis 'spider lily' form, with long, narrow, recurving petals surrounding a central, funnel-shaped corona with projecting stamens — are produced in umbels of 3–12 on tall, stout scapes, blooming in summer and fall. The fragrance is extraordinary — sweet, powerful, and vanilla-spice-like — and strongest in the evening, attracting hawk moths and nocturnal pollinators. Hymenocallis latifolia is distinguished from the freshwater-adapted H. littoralis by its preference for well-drained, coastal, limestone, and sandy soils rather than wet or flooded conditions, making it more versatile for typical garden settings. It shows excellent tolerance of salt spray, alkaline limestone soils, and sandy coastal substrates — making it outstanding for coastal Florida, Gulf Coast, and Caribbean garden planting where many bulbs fail.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Hymenocallis latifolia
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Florida Native Bulbs
